predictions.text: By 2026-09-30, the U.S. will impose Iran-related sanctions on at least one China-based entity (company or individual) explicitly for providing weapons, missiles, drones, or military components to Iran.
predictions.verification_criteria: A U.S. Treasury (OFAC) or U.S. State Department public designation/sanctions notice on an entity with location/incorporation in China (incl. Hong Kong) where the stated basis includes supplying weapons/missiles/drones/military components to Iran.
evidence: U.S. intelligence indicates China is taking a more active role supporting Iran’s war effort (missiles/supplies), raising escalation/sanctions risk: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/11/us/politics/china-iran-war-missiles-supplies.html
monitoring_signals: OFAC press releases and SDN list updates (Treasury.gov)
State Department sanctions announcements (state.gov)
Federal Register sanctions-related notices
Major wire coverage (Reuters/AP/Bloomberg) on China-Iran arms transfers
predictions.rationale: If China-linked transfers become more visible or attributable, sanctions are a common U.S. escalation tool short of direct confrontation.

predictions.text: By 2026-06-30, at least one major marine insurance broker/market body (Marsh, Aon, WTW, or Lloyd’s Market Association) will publicly report that the additional war-risk premium for a single transit of the Strait of Hormuz exceeds 0.50% of insured hull value for at least one class of commercial vessel.
predictions.verification_criteria: A public market update (PDF/report/web post) from Marsh/Aon/WTW/LMA stating a quoted additional war-risk premium (or equivalent war-risk surcharge) for Hormuz/Persian Gulf transit above 0.50% of hull value (or an explicitly comparable metric).
evidence: U.S. Navy described clearing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, highlighting acute chokepoint risk: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/11/us/politics/us-navy-warships-strait-of-hormuz-iran-mines.html
monitoring_signals: Marsh/Aon/WTW marine market updates
Lloyd’s Market Association and Joint War Committee publications
UKMTO/CMF incident feeds that precede pricing spikes
Shipping press (Lloyd’s List, TradeWinds) war-risk pricing reports
predictions.rationale: Mine/drone risk and uncertain de-escalation often translate quickly into higher war-risk premia.

predictions.text: By 2026-09-30, the University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers will report 1-year inflation expectations at or above 5.0% in at least one preliminary or final monthly release.
predictions.verification_criteria: Any U. Michigan consumer sentiment report (prelim or final) dated between 2026-04-12 and 2026-09-30 shows 1-year inflation expectations ≥ 5.0%.
evidence: Consumer sentiment hit a record low as inflation expectations rose amid the Iran war: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/10/consumer-sentiment-inflation-fears-iran-war.html
monitoring_signals: U. Michigan monthly releases (surveysofconsumers.org)
Gasoline price changes (AAA, EIA) as a driver of short-run expectations
CPI prints and inflation surprise indices
predictions.rationale: Energy shocks and heightened uncertainty can push short-run household expectations above prior ranges.

predictions.text: By 2026-08-31, Freddie Mac’s Primary Mortgage Market Survey (PMMS) will report the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage average at or below 6.00% in at least one weekly release.
predictions.verification_criteria: Any weekly Freddie Mac PMMS release through 2026-08-31 shows the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage average ≤ 6.00%.
evidence: Mortgage rates dipped after cease-fire news, improving affordability at the margin: https://www.marketwatch.com/story/mortgage-rates-dip-following-iran-cease-fire-why-thats-great-timing-for-people-selling-a-home-be81ad97
monitoring_signals: Freddie Mac PMMS weekly prints
10-year Treasury yield and MBS spread indicators
Fed communication and SOFR futures curve shifts
predictions.rationale: If markets continue pricing easing and term yields drift lower, mortgage rates can cross the 6% threshold.

predictions.text: By 2026-06-30, CME FedWatch (or equivalent CME-published probabilities) will show at least a 50% probability that the federal funds target range midpoint at the 2026-12-16 FOMC meeting is ≥75 bps lower than the midpoint in effect on 2026-04-09.
predictions.verification_criteria: A screenshot or archived CME FedWatch probabilities (or CME-published implied policy distribution) dated on/before 2026-06-30 shows P(midpoint Dec 2026 meeting ≤ baseline_midpoint(2026-04-09) - 0.75%) ≥ 50%.
evidence: Markets are pricing meaningful easing; analysis argues real yields suggest a potential 50-bp cut is coming: https://www.marketwatch.com/story/interest-rates-are-headed-lower-real-yields-suggest-a-half-point-fed-cut-is-coming-b5fd1e22
monitoring_signals: CME FedWatch tool (meeting-by-meeting probabilities)
SOFR futures and OIS-implied policy paths
Fed speakers and SEP updates
Inflation/employment surprise trends
predictions.rationale: If growth/inflation data soften or risk-off persists, the market may quickly price a deeper 2026 easing path.

predictions.text: By 2026-12-31, a USPS-related federal law will be enacted that provides at least $10 billion in net new near-term liquidity support via either (a) an appropriation/grant/transfer totaling ≥$10B to USPS or (b) an increase of the USPS borrowing authority by ≥$10B.
predictions.verification_criteria: A bill signed into law (Public Law) by 2026-12-31 whose text (or CBO summary) explicitly provides USPS ≥$10B in appropriated funds/transfers OR raises USPS borrowing limit by ≥$10B.
evidence: USPS is reported to be running out of money, implying policy action risk: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/11/us/politics/postal-service-finances-mail.html
monitoring_signals: Congress.gov USPS finance/appropriation bills
House/Senate committee markups affecting USPS funding
USPS Inspector General and GAO financial updates
Treasury/OMB statements on postal finances
predictions.rationale: A funding crunch plus operational mandates often force Congress to choose between liquidity support and service cuts.

predictions.text: By 2026-10-31, at least one of ARCC, OBDC, FSK, BXSL, or MAIN will announce a cut to its regular (non-special) dividend per share of at least 10% versus its immediately prior regular dividend, and will cite higher non-accruals, credit losses, or portfolio deterioration as a reason.
predictions.verification_criteria: An earnings release, 10-Q, or investor presentation for ARCC/OBDC/FSK/BXSL/MAIN announces a new regular dividend per share ≤ 90% of the prior regular dividend and includes explicit language attributing the cut to credit performance deterioration (e.g., non-accruals, defaults, realized losses, downgrades).
evidence: Investors are increasingly focused on private-credit stress and potential transmission into liquid vehicles: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/11/bond-market-private-credit-crisis-fixed-income-etfs.html
monitoring_signals: BDC quarterly earnings and dividend declarations
BDC non-accrual and NAV trend disclosures
Loan market default rates and recovery estimates
High-yield and leveraged-loan spread moves
predictions.rationale: If private-credit stress rises, larger BDCs may protect balance sheets through dividend cuts tied to credit quality.

predictions.text: By 2026-12-31, SiFive will publicly file an IPO registration statement (S-1 or F-1) on SEC EDGAR for a U.S. listing.
predictions.verification_criteria: SEC EDGAR shows a publicly accessible S-1 or F-1 filed by SiFive, Inc. (or a SiFive holding entity) for a U.S. IPO by 2026-12-31. Confidential submissions do not count unless made public on EDGAR.
evidence: Nvidia-backed SiFive hit a $3.65B valuation, signaling investor appetite for customizable silicon: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/11/nvidia-backed-sifive-hits-3-65-billion-valuation-for-open-ai-chips/
monitoring_signals: SEC EDGAR new registrant filings for SiFive
Hiring of underwriters and IPO press leaks (WSJ/Reuters/Bloomberg)
Company leadership statements on liquidity/markets
Semiconductor IPO window indicators
predictions.rationale: Strong AI-infra capital markets could reopen an IPO path, but timing remains uncertain.

predictions.text: By 2026-12-31, the U.S. Department of Commerce will initiate (announce or publish in the Federal Register) a Section 232 national security investigation that explicitly covers pharmaceutical products and/or active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs).
predictions.verification_criteria: A Commerce Department press release or Federal Register notice by 2026-12-31 explicitly states initiation of a Section 232 investigation and names pharmaceutical products and/or APIs as covered scope.
evidence: Policy shifts and trade pressure around pharma competitiveness are rising, affecting capital flows and trade policy: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/11/trump-pharma-tariffs-mfn-china-europe-biotech-stocks.html
monitoring_signals: Federal Register Section 232 notices
Commerce/Industry & Security Bureau announcements
White House/USTR trade investigations and tariff signaling
Congressional hearings on drug supply chain security
predictions.rationale: Supply-chain security framing makes pharma a plausible next target for national-security trade actions.

predictions.text: By 2026-10-31, the IMF will reduce its forecast for 2027 global real GDP growth by at least 0.2 percentage points in the October 2026 World Economic Outlook compared with the April 2026 WEO.
predictions.verification_criteria: IMF October 2026 WEO global growth forecast for 2027 (world real GDP growth, %) is at least 0.2 pp lower than the corresponding forecast published in the April 2026 WEO (as shown in WEO tables/database).
evidence: Diplomacy setbacks and renewed conflict risk imply ongoing commodity/FX volatility and growth headwinds: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/11/us-iran-talks-set-to-begin-in-islamabad-after-delegations-arrive.html
monitoring_signals: IMF WEO database updates (April vs October vintages)
Global PMI trends and trade volume data
Oil/shipping cost indices linked to Middle East disruptions
Major central bank forecast revisions
predictions.rationale: Persistent conflict risk and tighter financial conditions often lead the IMF to mark down forward global growth.
